explanation.text: Daisy has been persistently looking for the perfect word but is tired because she still has not found it. This context shows that her "quest" is a determined search.

The children give Miss Goldner many different presents, including purchased items, a handmade frame, and cards with personal messages. This variety supports the theme that people can show their care in many ways.

Paragraph 14 describes why "giggle" is enjoyable to say and hear. When Miss Goldner and the students try the word in paragraphs 19 through 22, its sound makes them smile and laugh, showing that Daisy's description causes the response.

Daisy presents the word with a flourish and asks Miss Goldner to experience it rather than simply accept the gift. The smiles and laughter that follow demonstrate that Daisy wants her teacher to discover how fun "giggle" is to say and hear.
Samantha initially calls Daisy's idea ridiculous and denies that a perfect word exists. By the end she says Daisy's word was the best gift, which reveals her change from scorn to appreciation.

One word spreads smiles and laughter through the entire class, draws in the principal, and even changes Samantha's opinion. These effects show the author's point that words can have considerable power.

Before David Greybeard enters camp, the chimpanzees always flee whenever Goodall approaches. His brave visit therefore marks an important change that allows her research relationship with the animals to begin improving.

Goodall repeatedly leaves bananas for David Greybeard and lets him visit without frightening him. His eventual willingness to take a banana from her hand shows that her patient actions build his trust.

David Greybeard begins approaching Goodall in the forest, and other chimpanzees copy his confidence and let her come closer. This success demonstrates that her thoughtful, patient effort to earn the animals' trust improves her study.

The article says Goodall "opened the door" for other women to become scientists who study animal behavior. This statement directly shows that her career created future opportunities for women researchers.

The article moves from David Greybeard's first camp visit through repeated feedings, growing trust, access to other chimpanzees, and Goodall's later discoveries. This chronological structure shows how each small development contributes to the success of her work.

The labeled turkey diagram points to the wishbone in the upper chest, between the neck and breast bone. This visual location supports paragraph 2's description of where the furcula sits in a bird's body.
The article traces wish-making on a bird's furcula back to the ancient Etruscans in northern Italy. That ancient origin directly supports the claim that pulling a wishbone dates back centuries.

The account begins with the ancient Etruscans, then follows the ritual to the Romans, the English, settlers in the New World, and modern Thanksgiving. This progression shows that the details are organized in the order the tradition developed over time.

The wishbone ritual passes from the Etruscans to Romans, then to the English and settlers in the New World, changing along the way. This history shows that many groups of people share in creating and continuing the tradition.

Paragraph 8 explains that greater pressure on one's side of the bone improves the chance of receiving the larger piece. This cause-and-effect advice most directly tells a reader how to increase the odds of winning.

The article chiefly traces wish-making from the ancient Etruscans through later cultures to today's Thanksgiving tug. The tradition's thousands-year history belongs in a summary because it captures the article's broad focus rather than a minor detail.
